narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 46.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. This decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your LSAT score, Year 3 school decile ranking, and Year 1 school decile ranking standing out as the most influential. Your LSAT score of 36, slightly below the typical 36.7 average, was the strongest positive factor—it pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass, showing the model sees real potential there despite the small dip. However, your Year 3 decile of 2, well below the average of 5.53, had an even larger counteracting effect, signaling to the model weaker performance relative to peers and strongly tilting toward a failure risk. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the average 5.71, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic struggles that the model links to bar exam challenges. Other elements like your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 (below the 3.21 average) and full-time student status played supporting roles but weren't as decisive. In essence, while your LSAT offered a foothold toward success, your lower decile rankings in school grades outweighed it, placing you below typical admitted students who often rank in the middle or higher deciles. This pattern reflects how the model weighs consistent academic standing heavily for bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile equivalents. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions advisors for targeted advice, and explore schools with holistic reviews that might value your full-time commitment and background more flexibly. With focused improvements, you can reshape this prediction in your favor.
